evals.name: author-functional-from-intent
evals.prompt: I want to add a requirement to the cerberus design-contract.md that captures this: data consumers should be able to retrieve data with classificationMarking containing only CAPCO and CUI government marking tokens — no PR- or DS- distribution tokens. This should hold across all consumer-facing surfaces (REST APIs, Secure Messaging REST, AODR/Bulk Data, SCS). Write the requirement in the design-contract format (#### R-N: Title, *Functional.* or *NFR.*, then the body). Pick a sensible R-N placeholder.
evals.expected_output: A single atomic requirement in user-story format wrapped in the cerberus design-contract shape. Should pick a real stakeholder (data consumer), state one verifiable behavior, and include a non-trivial so-that rationale. Should NOT bundle providers, should NOT use 'As the system', should NOT split into multiple requirements.

evals.name: author-nfr-stakeholder-discovery
evals.prompt: Need an NFR in the cerberus design contract about how the government controls mechanism (classification level, security controls, dissemination controls, releasability) shouldn't have a fixed capacity ceiling. The mechanism should scale with the distinct marking population. Context: today we have ~25 markings but SAP-level UDL would have markings-per-program with users holding hundreds of program PIDs across SCI compartments. Write the requirement in design-contract format.
evals.expected_output: A single atomic NFR in user-story format. Should run stakeholder discovery and pick something like 'platform architect' or 'capacity planner' — NOT 'the system'. The so-that should name the real consequence (SAP-level supportability, future-CUI accommodation). Should keep the requirement solution-neutral (don't dictate the mechanism, just the property).

evals.name: atomize-r23-multi-paragraph
evals.prompt:
Here's R-23 from cerberus-docs design-contract.md. I want to atomize it — it feels like multiple requirements bundled. Rewrite it as multiple atomic requirements in the design-contract format. Map the splits explicitly (e.g., R-23 splits into R-23a, R-23b, ...).

#### R-23: Preserve existing production integration behaviors

*Functional.*

The new model accounts for every behavior the existing custom production integrations support today. Each behavior is either preserved (the new model produces the same observable result) or explicitly migrated (the new model produces a documented, deliberate replacement). Nothing is dropped silently.

The custom NiFi integration is the canonical example: every content-routing rule, source reassignment, origin preservation, tag output, multi-step rule precedence, and default-fallback behavior must have a documented home in the new model. The same principle applies to every other in-production integration across the surface area.

Personas and scenarios serve as the behavioral contract the data model must fulfill. This requirement guarantees the contract is not quietly narrowed during the cutover. Whenever a scenario surfaces a behavior the existing integration supports, that behavior earns either a preservation answer or an explicit migration answer before any cutover.
evals.expected_output: Three atomic requirements (R-23a, R-23b, R-23c or similar): one for the general preservation-or-explicit-migration principle, one for the NiFi-specific behavior coverage, one for the persona/scenario contract guarantee. Each in user-story format with distinct actors where appropriate (integration partner for 23a/b, delivery lead for 23c). Each with its own so-that. Should NOT compress to fewer requirements. Should NOT collapse into one.

evals.name: atomize-r17-two-behaviors-one-paragraph
evals.prompt:
R-17 from the cerberus design contract feels like it's actually two things bundled — the access-gate ordering AND the cross-surface consistency of that ordering. Can you split it?

#### R-17: Classification gate evaluates first

*Functional.*

On any record carrying a `classificationMarking`, the Government Access Control gate evaluates first. UDL Access Control runs only on records that pass the classification gate. Conditional grants run on top of UDL Access Control. The ordering is consistent across every surface that performs an access decision (REST, RLS, Java ACM, Kafka SM, AODR / Bulk, SCS, NiFi).
evals.expected_output: Two atomic requirements (R-17a, R-17b or similar): one for the access-gate ordering itself (classification → UDL AC → conditional grants), one for cross-surface consistency of that ordering. Each in user-story format. Both could legitimately use 'security officer' as the actor. Each should be independently verifiable — should articulate the distinction (one could fail without the other failing).
